title: Multiply using partial products
description: Multiply 1-digit numbers by 2-, 3-, or 4-digit numbers by multiplying by the thousands, hundreds, tens and ones separately, then adding the products together.
topic_path: Topic C: Multiplication of up to four digits by single-digit numbers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $73\times 4$.**

|||
-:|-: | :-
|$73$ ||
|$\underline{{}\times 4}$ ||
Step 1| [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $4 \times $ [[☃ dropdown 1]]|
Step 2 |$\underline{{}+280}$ | $4 \times 70$|
Step 3 |[[☃ numeric-input 4]] ||
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $4$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
7\blueE{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}}&\\
\tealE{12}&\quad \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{3} \\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{7}3&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}}&\\
12&\quad {4} \times {3} \\
\underline{{}+\tealE{280}}&\quad \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{70} \\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
73&\\
\underline{{} \times 4}&\\
\purpleD{12}&\quad {4} \times {3} \\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{280}}&\quad {4} \times {70} \\
292
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
73&\\
\underline{{} \times 4}&\\
{12}&\quad {4} \times {3} \\
\underline{{}+{280}}&\quad {4} \times {70} \\
292
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $52\times 6$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$52$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 6}$ |
Step 1| [[☃ numeric-input 6]]| $6 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 2| $\underline{{}+12}$ | $6 \times 2$
Step 3| [[☃ numeric-input 4]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $6$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
52&\\
\underline{{} \times 6}&\\
\purpleD{300}&\quad{6} \times{50} \\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{12}}&\quad 6\times {2}\\
312
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
52&\\
\underline{{} \times 6}&\\
300&\quad{6} \times{50} \\
\underline{{}+12}&\quad 6\times {2}\\
312
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $826\times 3$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$826$ |
|$\underline{{}\times~~ 3}$ |
Step 1|$2{,}400$| $3 \times 800$
Step 2|[[☃ numeric-input 5]] |  $3 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 3|$\underline{{}+18}$| $3 \times 6$
Step 4|[[☃ numeric-input 4]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $3$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
82\blueE{6}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{3}}&\\
2{,}400&\quad 3 \times 800\\
60&\quad 3 \times 20\\
\underline{{}+\goldE{18}}&\quad \maroonE{3} \times \blueE{6}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
826&\\
\underline{{} \times 3}&\\
\purpleD{2{,}400}&\quad 3 \times 800\\
\purpleD{60}&\quad {3} \times {20} \\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{18}}&\quad {3} \times 6\\
2{,}478
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
826&\\
\underline{{} \times 3}&\\
{2{,}400}&\quad 3 \times 800\\
{60}&\quad {3} \times {20} \\
\underline{{}+{18}}&\quad {3} \times 6\\
2{,}478
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $391\times7$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$391$ |
|$\underline{{}\times~~ 7}$ | 
Step 1|$7$ |$7 \times 1$
Step 2|[[☃ numeric-input 5]]|  $7 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 3|$\underline{{}+2{,}100}$| $7 \times 300$
Step 4|[[☃ numeric-input 4]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $7$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{3}91&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{7}}&\\
7&\quad {7} \times 1 \\
{630}&\quad {7} \times {90} \\
\underline{{}+\goldE{2{,}100}}&\quad \maroonE{7} \times \blueE{300}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
391&\\
\underline{{} \times 7}&\\
\purpleD{7}&\quad {7} \times 1 \\
\purpleD{630}&\quad {7} \times {90}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{2{,}100}}&\quad {7} \times {300}\\
2{,}737
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
391&\\
\underline{{} \times 7}&\\
{7}&\quad {7} \times 1 \\
{630}&\quad {7} \times {90}\\
\underline{{}+{2{,}100}}&\quad {7} \times {300}\\
2{,}737
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $2{,}104\times8$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$2{,}104$ |
|$\underline{{}\times~~~~ 8}$ | 
Step 1|$32$ |$8 \times 4$
Step 2|$0$ | $8\times0$
Step 3|[[☃ numeric-input 4]]|  $8 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 4|$\underline{{}+16{,}000}$| $8 \times 2{,}000$
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 5]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $8$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
2{,}\blueE{1}04&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE{8}}&\\
32&\quad {8} \times {4}\\
0&\quad{8} \times{0}\\
\goldE{800}&\quad \maroonE{8} \times \blueE{100}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{2}{,}104&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE{8}}&\\
32&\quad {8} \times {4}\\
0&\quad{8} \times{0}\\
800&\quad {8} \times {100}\\
\underline{{}+\goldE{16{,}000}}&\quad \maroonE{8} \times \blueE{2{,}000}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
2{,}104&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ 8}&\\
\purpleD{32}&\quad {8} \times {4}\\
\purpleD{0}&\quad{8} \times{0}\\
\purpleD{800}&\quad {8} \times {100}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{16{,}000}}&\quad {8} \times {2{,}000}\\
16{,}832
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
2{,}104&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ 8}&\\
{32}&\quad {8} \times {4}\\
{0}&\quad{8} \times{0}\\
{800}&\quad {8} \times {100}\\
\underline{{}+{16{,}000}}&\quad {8} \times {2{,}000}\\
16{,}832
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $4{,}955\times2$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$4{,}955$ |
|$\underline{{}\times ~~~~2}$ | 
Step 1|$8{,}000$| $2 \times4{,}000$
Step 2|$1{,}800$| $2\times 900$
Step 3|[[☃ numeric-input 9]] | $2\times{}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 4|$\underline{{}+10}$ |$2 \times 5$
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 8]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $2$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
4{,}9\blueE{5}5&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE{2}}&\\
8{,}000&\quad 2 \times 4{,}000\\
1{,}800&\quad {2} \times {900}\\
\goldE{100}&\quad \maroonE{2} \times \blueE{50}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
4{,}95\blueE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE{2}}&\\
8{,}000&\quad 2 \times 4{,}000\\
1{,}800&\quad {2} \times {900}\\
100&\quad{2} \times{50}\\
\underline{{}+\goldE{10}}&\quad \maroonE{2} \times \blueE{5}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
4{,}955&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ 2}&\\
\purpleD{8{,}000}&\quad {2} \times {4{,}000}\\
\purpleD{1{,}800}&\quad {2} \times {900}\\
\purpleD{100}&\quad{2} \times{50}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{10}}&\quad {2} \times {5}\\
9{,}910
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
4{,}955&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ 2}&\\
{8{,}000}&\quad {2} \times {4{,}000}\\
{1{,}800}&\quad {2} \times {900}\\
{100}&\quad{2} \times{50}\\
\underline{{}+{10}}&\quad {2} \times {5}\\
9{,}910
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Erin tried to evaluate $48\times2$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$
\begin{align}
&&48 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}2} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}\quad&\blueE{8}&& \qquad\blueE{2\times 40}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 2}}&\underline\purpleE{{}+\phantom{2} 16}& &\qquad\purpleE{2\times 8}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{24}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Erin's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Erin broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{4}8&\\
\underline{{} \times \blueE{2}}&\\
\blueE{80}&\quad \blueE{2} \times \blueE{40}
\end{align}$

Erin said that $\blueE{2}\times\blueE{40}$ equals $\blueE{8}$. So Erin made a mistake in step $1$.
Erin made a mistake in step 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
Molly tried to evaluate $93\times5$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$
\begin{align}
&&93 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}5} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}\quad&\blueE{150}&& \qquad\blueE{5\times30}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 2}}&\underline\purpleE{{}+\phantom{2} 450}& &\quad\purpleE{5\times 90}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{600}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Molly's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Molly broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
9\blueE{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times \blueE{5}}&\\
\blueE{150}&\quad \blueE{5} \times \blueE{30}
\end{align}$

Molly said that there were $3$ tens, not $3$ ones, in $9\blueE{3}$. So Molly made a mistake in step $1$.
Molly made a mistake in step 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kylar tried to evaluate $508\times4$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$\begin{align}
&&508 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}4} \\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 1}}&\maroonE{\phantom{2}{2{,}000}}&\qquad\maroonE{4\times 500}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 2}}&\purpleE{{}\phantom{2} 40} &\qquad\purpleE{4\times 0}\\
&\blueE{\text{Step 3}}\quad&\underline{\blueE{{}+32}}&\qquad\blueE{4\times8}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\goldE{2{,}072}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Kylar's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Kylar broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{5}08&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}}&\\
\maroonE{\phantom{2}{2{,}000}}&\qquad\maroonE{4\times 500}\\
\end{align}$

There are $5$ hundreds in $508$, and $\maroonE{4}\times\maroonE{500}=\maroonE{2{,}000}$. So Kylar did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
5\purpleE{0}8&\\
\underline{{} \times \purpleE{4}}&\\
2{,}000&\quad {4} \times {500} \\
\purpleE{0}&\quad \purpleE{4} \times \purpleE{0\text{ tens}}
\end{align}$

Kylar said that $\purpleE4\times\purpleE{0}$ equals $\purpleE{40}$. So Kylar made a mistake in step $2$.
Kylar made a mistake in step $2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joe tried to evaluate $612\times8$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$
\begin{align}
&&612 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}8} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}\quad&\blueE{16}&\qquad\blueE{8\times2}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 2}}&\purpleE{{}\phantom{2} 80} &\qquad\purpleE{8\times 10}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\underline\maroonE{+\phantom{2}{480}}&\qquad\maroonE{8\times 60}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\goldE{576}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Joe's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Joe broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
61\blueE{2}&\\
\underline{{} \times \blueE{8}}&\\
\blueE{16}&\quad \blueE{8} \times \blueE{2}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ ones in $612$, and $\blueE8\times\blueE2=\blueE{16}$. So Joe did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
6\purpleE{1}2&\\
\underline{{} \times \purpleE{8}}&\\
16&\quad {8} \times {2} \\
{{}\purpleE{80}}&\quad \purpleE{8} \times \purpleE{10}
\end{align}$

There is $1$ ten in $612$, and $\purpleE8\times\purpleE{10}=\purpleE{80}$. So Joe did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
\maroonE612&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE2}&\\
{16}&\qquad{8\times 2}\\
{80}&\qquad {8} \times 10\\
\underline{{}+\maroonE{4{,}800}}&\qquad\maroonE{8\times 600}
\end{align}$

Joe said that there were $6$ tens, not $6$ hundreds, in $\maroonE{6}12$. So Joe made a mistake in step $3$.
Joe made a mistake in step $3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Denny tried to evaluate $3{,}479\times7$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$\begin{align}
&&3{,}479 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}7} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}\quad&\blueE{63}&\qquad\blueE{7\times9}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 2}}&\tealE{{}\phantom{2} 490} &\qquad\tealE{7\times 70}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{\phantom{2}{2{,}800}}&\qquad\maroonE{7\times 400}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline\goldE{+\phantom{2}{2{,}100}}&\qquad\goldE{7\times 3{,}000}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{5{,}453}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Denny's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Denny broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
3{,}47\blueE{9}&\\
\underline{{} \times \blueE{7}}&\\
\blueE{63}&\quad \blueE{7} \times \blueE{9}
\end{align}$

There are $9$ ones in $3{,}479$, and $\blueE7\times\blueE9=\blueE{63}$. So Denny did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
3{,}4\tealE{7}9&\\
\underline{{} \times ~~~\tealE{7}}&\\
63&\quad {7} \times {9} \\
{{}\tealE{490}}&\quad \tealE{7} \times \tealE{70}
\end{align}$

There are $7$ tens in $3{,}479$, and $\tealE7\times\tealE{70}=\tealE{490}$. So Denny did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
3{,}\maroonE479&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE7}&\\
{63}&\qquad{7\times 9}\\
{490}&\qquad {7} \times 70\\
{{}\maroonE{2{,}800}}&\qquad\maroonE{7\times 400}
\end{align}$

There are $4$ hundreds in $3{,}479$, and $\maroonE{7}\times\maroonE{400}=\maroonE{2{,}800}$. So Denny did not make a mistake in step $3$.

###Step $4$

$\begin{align}
\goldE3{,}479&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \goldE7}&\\
{63}&\qquad{7\times 9}\\
{490}&\qquad {7} \times 70\\
{{}{2{,}800}}&\qquad{7\times 400}\\
\underline{+~\goldE{21{,}000}}&\qquad{\goldE{7\times3{,}000}}
\end{align}$

Denny said that $\goldE{7}\times\goldE{3{,}000}$ equals $\goldE{2{,}100}$, so Denny made a make a mistake in step $4$.
Denny made a mistake in step $4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lori tried to evaluate $8{,}092\times6$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$\begin{align}
&&8{,}092 \\
&&\underline{\times\phantom{22}6} \\
&\goldE{\text{Step 1}}&\goldE{{48{,}000}}&\qquad\goldE{6\times 8{,}000}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 2}}&\maroonE{{0}}&\qquad\maroonE{6\times 0}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 3}}&\tealE{540} &\qquad\tealE{6\times 90}\\
&\blueE{\text{Step 4}}\quad&\underline{\blueE{{}+12}}&\qquad\blueE{6\times2}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{552}& &
 \end{align}$

**Find Lori's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Lori broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\goldE8{,}092&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \goldE6}&\\
\goldE{48{,}000}&\qquad{\goldE{6\times8{,}000}}
\end{align}$

There are $8$ thousands in $8{,}092$, and $\goldE{6}\times\goldE{8{,}000}=\goldE{48{,}000}$. So Lori did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
8{,}\maroonE092&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ \maroonE6}&\\
{48{,}000}&\qquad{{6\times8{,}000}}\\
\maroonE{0}&\qquad\maroonE{6\times 0}
\end{align}$

There are $0$ hundreds in $8{,}092$, and $\maroonE{6}\times\maroonE{0}=\maroonE{0}$. So Lori did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
8{,}0\tealE{9}2&\\
\underline{{} \times ~~~\tealE{6}}&\\
{48{,}000}&\qquad{{6\times8{,}000}}\\
{{}{0}}&\qquad{6\times 0}\\
{{}\tealE{540}}&\qquad \tealE{6} \times \tealE{90}
\end{align}$

There are $9$ tens in $8{,}092$, and $\tealE6\times\tealE{90}=\tealE{540}$. So Lori did not make a mistake in step $3$.

###Step $4$

$\begin{align}
8{,}09\blueE{2}&\\
\underline{{} \times \blueE{6}}&\\
{48{,}000}&\qquad{{6\times8{,}000}}\\
{{}{0}}&\qquad{6\times 0}\\
{540}&\qquad {6} \times 90\\
\underline{{}+\blueE{12}}&\qquad \blueE{6} \times \blueE{2}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ ones in $8{,}092$, and $\blueE6\times\blueE2=\blueE{12}$. So Lori did not make a mistake in step $4$.

###Step $5$

$\begin{align}
8{,}092&\\
\underline{{} \times~~~ 6}&\\
\purpleE{48{,}000}&\qquad{{6\times8{,}000}}\\
\purpleE{{}{0}}&\qquad{6\times 0}\\
\purpleE{540}&\qquad {6} \times 90\\
\underline{\purpleE{{}+12}}&\qquad{6\times 2}\\
\purpleE{48{,}552} 
\end{align}$

Lori said that $\purpleE{48{,}000 + 0 + 540 + 12}$ equals $\purpleE{552}$, so Lori made a mistake in step $5$.
Lori made a mistake in step $5$.
